theoretical_prediction: Operator performance degrades after 20 minutes on task
observed_unit: flight duration as proxy for continuous monitoring load
thesis_implication: Average flight time of 172s (2.9 min) is well below the 20-min Mackworth threshold. Individual missions likely don't trigger vigilance decrement, but cumulative shift monitoring (8-12 hrs across 10,000+ missions) does. The cognitive load is from continuous feed monitoring between flights, not individual flights.

theoretical_prediction: 75% of SA failures are comprehension-level (Level 2), not detection (Level 1)
observed_value: 234 sec avg response time for P1 calls
observed_unit: response time measures aerial arrival (SA Level 1)
thesis_implication: 234s response time is fast for aerial detection (SA Level 1). No data on whether operators correctly comprehend situations (Level 2) or predict outcomes (Level 3). Endsley predicts 75% of SA failures occur at Level 2 — the 'seeing but not understanding' problem. Power BI dashboard tracks missions but not decision quality.

observed_value: Expanded from downtown to city-wide coverage
thesis_implication: Chula Vista expanded from downtown to broader city coverage. Early concentration in downtown (higher-crime, lower-income areas) aligns with Tyler's prediction of perceived bias. Expansion may mitigate legitimacy concerns by distributing surveillance more equitably. No quantitative spatial data available — FOIA request recommended.

theoretical_prediction: Humans attribute intent to ambiguous movement patterns (~70% accuracy)
observed_unit: mission diversity as proxy for attribution demand
thesis_implication: With 10,000+ missions, operators have repeatedly interpreted ambiguous aerial footage. Heider-Simmel predicts ~70% accuracy in intent attribution from movement alone. Aerial perspective amplifies ambiguity — distance, angle, and resolution reduce behavioral cues. The 'God's eye view' may create false confidence in intent assessments.

theoretical_prediction: Repeated exposure produces automatic threat evaluation within 250ms
observed_unit: deployment volume as proxy for operator experience
thesis_implication: With 10,000+ missions over 4 years, Chula Vista operators have the highest exposure of any DFR program. Bargh's automaticity predicts operators have developed automatic threat evaluations (<250ms activation). This speeds response but introduces systematic bias risk — experienced operators may default to learned patterns rather than deliberate analysis. Note: Bargh et al. (1996) behavioral priming findings failed replication (Doyen et al., 2012); automaticity of evaluation (Ferguson & Bargh, 2004) remains well-supported.

theoretical_prediction: Humans segment continuous experience into discrete events; accuracy degrades with sustained monitoring
observed_value: LTE real-time video streaming, autonomous flight transitions
observed_unit: video feed continuity as segmentation demand
thesis_implication: Continuous LTE video streaming creates an unbroken perceptual stream that requires operators to segment into meaningful events. Autonomous flight transitions (approach, hover, orbit, return) create natural event boundaries. Zacks predicts segmentation quality determines what operators remember — poor boundary detection leads to missed incidents or false alarms. Autonomous flight may help by creating salient transition cues.
